title: Top 10 Movies
description: A personal countdown of my favorite movies.

items.title: Avengers: Endgame
items.comment: One of only 3 movies I've seen 4x in theaters. Legendary performance by RDJ. Can't write it any better. Satisfying conclusion to an epic 11 year movie run. Sadly, MCU went downhill fast after this.

items.title: Office Space
items.comment: Only comedy on this list. Hits so close to home for anyone in corporate America. Really makes you wonder why we put up with the BS we do (bosses, meetings, memos, bills). Great soundtrack too.

items.title: Fight Club
items.quote: I'm about to break the first rule
items.comment: Unoriginal and probably on many top-10 lists. But that's for a reason. I recently saw this in theaters and it's aged quite well. Another movie from 1999 that really showcases how fucked up we've become. Slavery to corporate America for materialistic bs.

items.title: Mission: Impossible – Ghost Protocol
items.quote: Hate him or love him he's on top
items.comment: My favourite film franchise with none other than Mister Movie Star himself. Greatest action franchise no doubt. Hard to pick just one MI movie for the top 10, but I think this has a good balance of everything in it. Still the most fun. Cruise is why we go to the movies. No CGI bullshit, he's on top of the Burj.

items.title: Se7en
items.quote: JOHN DOE HAS THE UPPER HAND!
items.comment: Hard to resist not having Se7en as 7, but it's just too good. Gets better every time I watch it. Intro titles scene is the best of all time with remixed version of NIN "Closer". Head in the box and sloth are overrated. The lust scene and aftermath is really what gets to me. Start of Pitt being an A-list actor. And of course, Kevin Spacey as the uncredited John Doe. Kind of movie you watch to get better at filmmaking. Beautiful masterpiece, looks like it could've been made today.

items.title: Halloween
items.comment: Best horror theme of all time. The Shape. The Body. An intro to Jamie Lee Curtis who would end up being one of the greatest actresses of all time. Set precedent for slasher flicks despite having very little gore. It's the subtleties that make Michael and this movie so terrifying.

items.title: The Dark Knight
items.comment: Saw this 3x in theaters on opening day. And 5x overall which is my record. Best crime movie ever. Best villain ever and one of the best supporting acting roles of all time. Features my personal favorite actor Christian Bale in one of his best performances. Opening scene over Gotham skyscrapers in IMAX was one of the most memorable theater moments of all time. RIP Heath.

items.title: Toy Story 3
items.quote: You have got a friend in me
items.comment: Another unforgettable IMAX experience. Only movie on this list I've never rewatched (and don't have any intention to. Original watching was that magical). Shot above is about as terrifying as a children's film will get. Everyone (even Buzz) looking to Woody for an answer, as he just stares straight into Death. I watched this right before going off to college. So of course, ending hits home little hard and I don't even want to write about it.

items.title: The Social Network
items.comment: Aaron Sorkin's Oscar winning screenplay adaptation from Ben Mezrich's The Accidental Billionaires (based loosely on real-life events). Ha. I was absolutely obsessed with this movie in college for several reasons. Studying computer engineering in 2010 made everyone think they'd have the next college dorm room billion dollar idea. HA! My favorite musician Trent Reznor scored his first film and won the Oscar for best score. I'm also clearly a big Fincher guy too. This movie is dark af. Takes a story of some nerds and makes it really haunting. You finish the movie feeling kind of like "wtf did I just watch". Phenomal performances by the 2 lead actors ofc.

items.title: Spider-Man 2
items.comment: A lot of people come up to me and say, "Hey Saroj, you're a movie guy. What's your favorite movie of all time?" And unashamedly, I say Spider-Man 2. And they go, "Really? The old one with Tobey Maguire?" And I go, "Yeah." And the conversation kind of just ends there. Now, when I'm talking to other actual movie people, I always get an "Ooh, good choice." And I think there's a reason for that. I truly believe it's the perfect movie. Sure, there's nostalgia. There's nothing quite like being an 11-year-old in the theater in late June watching this on opening day. But it's a little bit of everything. It's the perfect hero storyline. You have a guy trying to get the girl while ending up fighting one of his genius mentors and his best friend who thinks he's betrayed him. He loses everything. He loses his powers, loses the girl to some hunk, decides he doesn't want to be the hero anymore... and then becomes, well, vindicated. Everyone knows the train scene is one of the best action sequences of all time. But if you actually go back and rewatch the movie, it's not just the train scene. There's a full 15 minutes before and after it that are absolutely brutal for a PG-13 film. This is still the most stylish action I've ever seen, and so little relies on cheap CGI. These films look ten times better than the new Tom Holland ones. It's just satisfying. Satisfying is the word I'd use to describe the action. Then at the end Peter reveals his identity to MJ, and has to sacrifice his love to be Spider-Man. Heartbreaking. Such a satisifying scene to see MJ run from wedding and risk everything to be with Peter. Leading to the iconic 'Go get 'em, Tiger' and the ending shot of Kirsten Dunst knowing she's risking everything to be with him. The ending is still so uncertain. Is Harry going to become the Goblin? There's just so many emotions that come out of this film. There's the iconic soundtrack as well. To me it's still number one, and I think it'll be a long time before that ever changes.
